evidence: README.md defines explicit runtime contracts for error handling notifier registration, Supabase client ownership, and legacy-proxy isolation, giving a clear architectural policy surface.

src/shared/lib/errorHandling/handleError.ts enforces notifier ownership with a single registration slot and an explicit guard against reconfiguration with a different notifier, aligning with startup-only wiring.

src/integrations/supabase/client.ts centralizes Supabase client creation behind getSupabaseClient() with lazy singleton behavior and keeps debug globals opt-in and dev-gated, matching README runtime scope claims.

src/shared/components/ui/button.tsx and src/shared/lib/uiClassNames.ts keep UI primitives thin and role-focused (policy composition and class merge utility), avoiding ownership bleed into data/runtime layers.

src/domains/generation/types/generationRow.ts shows intentional compatibility layering (legacy aliases and DB-field variants) but keeps it localized to a domain type contract rather than scattered across runtime modules.
high_level_elegance.unreported_risk: README runtime contracts are manually maintained; without an automated quality gate (contract tests or lint rule for forbidden legacy imports), docs-vs-runtime drift can reappear as the codebase evolves.

evidence: Task payload construction is spread across large param-bag builders with many conditional spreads and legacy compatibility branches (`src/shared/lib/tasks/travelBetweenImages/payloadBuilder.ts`, `src/shared/lib/tasks/joinClips.ts`, `src/shared/lib/tasks/individualTravelSegment.ts`, `src/shared/lib/tasks/magicEdit.ts`).

Multiple wrappers mainly forward context/props with little semantic translation, e.g. `useButtonGroupProps` copies shared fields into four almost-identical objects and is consumed by `LightboxLayout`.

There are broad orchestrator hooks that aggregate many sub-hooks and expose very large return contracts (`src/tools/edit-images/hooks/useInlineEditState.ts`, `src/shared/components/MediaLightbox/ImageLightbox.tsx`).

Edge boundary helpers expose multiple incompatible handoff contracts: `parseJsonBodyLoose` silently coerces invalid/array JSON to `{}` while `parseJsonBodyStrict` returns `{ errorResponse }` (`supabase/functions/_shared/edgeRequest.ts`).

`handleStorageOperations` mixes exception throwing for primary upload failure with embedded `EdgeOperationResult` for thumbnail degradation (`supabase/functions/complete_task/storage.ts`), forcing callers to handle both try/catch and status-object paths.

Rate-limit config carries `identifierType`, but `checkRateLimit` uses only a caller-provided `identifier` string and never branches on `identifierType` (`supabase/functions/_shared/rateLimit.ts`), so boundary behavior implied by config is not enforced by the integration seam.

`useLineageChain` claims full lineage traversal but only loads variants from the current `generation_id`, so cross-generation ancestry chains truncate silently (`src/shared/hooks/useLineageChain.ts`).

Legacy migration fields are still embedded in active-facing types via broad intersections (`ProjectImageSettings & Partial<LegacyProjectImageSettings>`), which keeps dual-schema handling alive instead of bounded to a decode boundary.

Travel-between-images types retain legacy-only fields directly on UI runtime objects (`StructureVideoConfigWithMetadata` includes `motion_strength`, `structure_type`, `uni3c_*`), increasing local branching and payload-shape ambiguity.

Storage completion flow contains multiple migration leftovers (`_isServiceRole`, `_sourceBytes`, `_userId`, `_taskId`, deprecated alias export) and recomputes `objectPath` in thumbnail flow rather than threading it through.
Task-type categorization claims centralized strictness but still uses substring heuristic (`taskType.includes('travel')`), introducing imprecise low-level control flow.

Two central UI hooks (`useImageEditOrchestrator`, `useTimelineOrchestrator`) each combine state ownership, effect timing, adapter logic, and large context/view-model assembly in single files (549 and 676 LOC), with very broad input/output contracts.

`useGenerationController` and `useEditingController` require and forward very wide parameter surfaces, then fan those values into multiple downstream hooks and return large merged objects, indicating controller-as-aggregation rather than focused responsibilities.

Image generation submission flow (`useFormSubmission` -> `useSubmissionHandlers` -> `usePromptQueueHandlers`) repeatedly threads overlapping prop sets through wrapper layers with limited local transformation, increasing indirection without clear ownership boundaries.

Edge functions (`stripe-checkout`, `process-auto-topup`) and completion handler (`generation-child.ts`) mix transport/auth/validation/rate-limiting/business rules/persistence in single entry modules, mirroring the same orchestration concentration pattern across backend code.

complete_task routing code types key inputs as unknown but dereferences them directly (`taskData.params`, `taskData.category`, `existingGeneration.id`) without a validated narrowing boundary in `supabase/functions/complete_task/generation.ts`.

Realtime instrumentation force-casts SDK internals (`supabase.realtime as unknown as RealtimeClientWithInstrumentation`) and mutates private fields (`socket`, `conn.transport`) in `src/integrations/supabase/instrumentation/realtime/index.ts`, so compile-time checks cannot detect SDK shape drift.

Cross-module payload contracts are frequently modeled as `Record<string, unknown>` and then re-cast (`buildOrchestrationContract`, `payloadBuilder` normalization casts, `edgeRequest` clientOptions cast), which shifts correctness to runtime and encourages downstream unsafe assumptions.

React Query cache access paths use `unknown` and ad-hoc structural assumptions (`query.queryKey as unknown[]`, `query.state.data as PaginatedResponse`, `oldData: unknown` filtered as `{id:string}` arrays) in preloading and shot-editor callbacks.

findings.summary: Central error utility owns UI notification wiring through global mutable state.
evidence: `handleError.ts` stores `registeredNotifier` at module scope and mutates it via `configureErrorNotifier`, while `handleError` conditionally emits notifications.

Callers in `useMobile.tsx` and `useMediaGalleryHandlers.ts` invoke `handleError` from domain/UI hooks, so behavior depends on external singleton configuration rather than explicit dependency at boundary.
findings.suggestion: Split error normalization/logging from notification transport: keep `handleError` pure (return structured error event), and inject notification handling at app composition boundaries (provider or explicit callback wrapper) instead of global module state.

findings.summary: Gallery preloader hook mixes feature orchestration with cache and transport policy ownership.
evidence: `useAdjacentPagePreloader` performs three distinct responsibilities: query prefetch strategy, preloading queue management, and query cache eviction.
`useMediaGalleryHandlers` is another broad orchestration hook in the same feature area, indicating behavior is accumulating in feature hooks instead of dedicated boundary services.
findings.suggestion: Extract a dedicated gallery prefetch coordinator service (policy + cache lifecycle) and keep `useAdjacentPagePreloader` as a thin hook adapter that only binds React lifecycle to that service.

findings.summary: Core generation types collapse storage, legacy, and UI concerns into single shared contracts.
evidence: `GenerationRow` includes parallel alias fields and mixed naming conventions (`thumbUrl`/`thumbnail_url`, `createdAt`/`created_at`, `shotImageEntryId`/`shot_generation_id`) plus legacy comments in one interface.
`Shot` directly references `GenerationRow[]` and includes both DB-like snake_case fields and UI-derived counters, indicating domain model and transport/read-model concerns are co-located.
findings.suggestion: Define separate boundary models (`DbGenerationRow`, `DomainGeneration`, `GalleryGenerationView`) and isolate translation in one mapper layer; migrate consumers to domain/view types and keep DB aliases out of shared domain interfaces.

findings.summary: Task orchestration identity is passed through inconsistent field paths, forcing fallback-heavy seam logic across client and edge layers.
evidence: Payload builders emit orchestration identifiers in mixed locations (`orchestration_contract`, top-level ids, nested `orchestrator_details`).
Completion code queries sibling tasks by multiple JSON paths with OR predicates and fallback strategy (run_id paths, orchestrator_task_id paths).
Generation routing then re-extracts parent/child/based_on semantics via a separate params extractor layer.
findings.suggestion: Define a single canonical orchestration envelope schema (shared TS + edge contract) with one source path per identity field, then migrate builders and completion queries to only that schema.

findings.summary: Lightbox control prop-building adds indirection cost while performing mostly mechanical field forwarding.
evidence: `useButtonGroupProps` duplicates shared context injection in four builders with near-identical field sets.
The hook memoizes on `[params]`, so upstream object identity changes can negate memo value.
`LightboxLayout` still consumes broad prop objects and context hooks, so this layer does not substantially reduce interface complexity.
findings.suggestion: Replace builder fan-out with one typed `LightboxControlsContext` object consumed directly by button groups, and keep per-group additions local at callsites where behavior differs.

findings.summary: Large orchestration hooks accumulate many responsibilities and return oversized state contracts, reducing local clarity.
evidence: `useInlineEditState` coordinates environment, persistence, inpainting, magic edit, reposition, img2img, source generation, star toggling, and model assembly in one hook.
Callers consume very wide nested return trees (`canvasEnvironment`, `inpaintingState`, `transformState`, `generationState`, etc.), which increases coupling.
Similar orchestration-heavy composition exists in image lightbox and shot image manager container, suggesting a recurring pattern.
findings.suggestion: Split by lifecycle boundary (data loading, edit-mode state machine, action commands, presentational selectors) and expose narrower hook APIs per panel/view.

findings.summary: Task payload modules rely on very large optional parameter bags with mutation-heavy assembly, obscuring stable contracts.
evidence: Each module defines broad params interfaces with many optional compatibility fields and mixed legacy/current concerns.
Payload construction uses long conditional assignment blocks and ad-hoc normalization helpers.
Similar patterns repeat across task families, but without one shared typed composition strategy.
findings.suggestion: Introduce per-task discriminated input modes (legacy/current, basic/advanced, single/batch) and compile to a strict canonical payload via shared composable builders.

findings.summary: Realtime instrumentation crosses module boundaries via global monkey-patching, creating brittle integration seams.
evidence: Instrumentation rewires `console.warn` and internal `realtime.socket`/`conn.transport` properties through `Object.defineProperty` interception.
Polling behavior and fetch-failure backoff are managed elsewhere in `DataFreshnessManager`, so stability logic spans disconnected mechanisms.
`useTasks` depends on this ecosystem via freshness callbacks and polling config, coupling query behavior to hidden runtime patches.
findings.suggestion: Move to explicit realtime health adapters (event emitters/hooks) instead of monkey-patches, and centralize connection-state transitions in one observable contract used by both instrumentation and query polling.

findings.summary: JSON request parsing has divergent failure semantics across the same edge seam.
evidence: `parseJsonBodyLoose` catches parse errors and returns `{}` (`edgeRequest.ts`), including when payload is invalid JSON or a non-object array via `asObject`.
`parseJsonBodyStrict` returns `{ errorResponse }` with 400 on parse failure (`edgeRequest.ts`), creating two incompatible caller contracts for the same request-boundary concern.
The shared `EdgeOperationResult` model already exists (`edgeOperation.ts`) but parsing helpers do not use it, so handlers must branch on ad-hoc shapes.
findings.suggestion: Unify body parsing to one contract (prefer `EdgeOperationResult<Record<string, unknown>>`), then require handlers to explicitly opt into fail-open defaults instead of receiving `{}` implicitly.

findings.summary: Storage completion flow mixes thrown errors with result-object failures in one API.
evidence: `handleStorageOperations` throws on main upload failure (`throw new Error(...)`), but returns `thumbnailOutcome: EdgeOperationResult` for thumbnail failures (`storage.ts`).
Callers must combine exception handling and `.ok` inspection for one boundary call, increasing orchestration complexity and missed-path risk.
`edgeOperation.ts` already defines consistent failure metadata (`policy`, `recoverable`, `errorCode`) that primary upload failures currently bypass.
findings.suggestion: Return `EdgeOperationResult<StorageResult>` from `handleStorageOperations` and encode primary upload failures with `edgeFailure(...)` so all storage outcomes follow one handoff contract.

findings.summary: Rate-limit config advertises identifier strategy that runtime path does not enforce.
evidence: `RateLimitConfig` includes `identifierType: 'ip' | 'user' | 'combined'` (`rateLimit.ts`), but `checkRateLimit` ignores it and only uses `identifier` string provided by caller.
Because the function key is always `${functionName}:${identifier}`, policy differences in `identifierType` never affect behavior in this shared seam.
This pushes identifier selection into each endpoint callsite, creating hidden coupling and inconsistent API behavior across handlers.
findings.suggestion: Move identifier derivation into the rate-limit module (accept request/auth context and `identifierType`), so presets are executable policy rather than documentation-only metadata.

findings.summary: Lineage API claims full ancestry but only resolves within one generation boundary.
evidence: `useLineageChain` documents “full lineage chain” but fetches only variants where `generation_id === currentVariant.generation_id` before traversal.
Traversal stops when parent `source_variant_id` is not in that in-memory map, producing a silent partial chain rather than explicit incomplete status.
`generationTransformers` exposes lineage-related metadata (`based_on`) across records, indicating cross-record ancestry is expected in surrounding integration flows.
findings.suggestion: Change lineage fetch to iterative parent lookups by `source_variant_id` (or a recursive SQL/RPC), and return an explicit `isPartial`/error state when ancestry cannot be fully resolved.

findings.summary: Legacy migration fields are kept in main runtime type surfaces, so migration debt remains in normal code paths.
evidence: `ProjectImageSettingsInput` is defined as `ProjectImageSettings & Partial<LegacyProjectImageSettings>` (lines 38-40), which permanently broadens accepted payload shape.

`StructureVideoConfigWithMetadata` includes legacy-only fields marked as migration decoding metadata (`motion_strength`, `structure_type`, `uni3c_start_percent`, `uni3c_end_percent`) in lines 124-131.
findings.suggestion: Move legacy fields into explicit one-way decoder input types and emit a strictly modern normalized type immediately; keep runtime/state interfaces free of deprecated keys.

findings.summary: Storage completion code contains dead migration parameters and compatibility aliases that obscure the active execution path.
evidence: `handleStorageOperations` accepts `_isServiceRole` but never uses it (line 44).
`generateThumbnail` accepts `_sourceBytes`, `_userId`, `_taskId` but never uses them (lines 165-167).
Deprecated alias `getStoragePublicUrl` remains exported (lines 216-219), extending old call paths.
Thumbnail transform path is recomputed in `handleThumbnail` (line 146) instead of receiving already-computed `objectPath` from caller.
findings.suggestion: Remove unused parameters and deprecated alias after call-site migration, pass `objectPath` through the call chain directly, and keep the storage path contract in one canonical shape.

findings.summary: Task type classification falls back to substring matching, making control flow less precise than the declared registry model.
evidence: Utility documentation positions set-based centralization, but `isTravelTaskType` still returns true on `taskType.includes('travel')` (line 47).
A structured fallback registry already exists in task config (`getTaskTypeConfigFallback` usage, lines 57-61), so heuristic classification is avoidable.
findings.suggestion: Replace substring fallback with explicit registry entries (or a typed enum map) and fail closed for unknown task types until added to the registry.

findings.summary: Deletion hook combines mutation orchestration with inline component factory creation, adding avoidable indirection and state coupling.
evidence: `useDeleteGenerationWithConfirm` returns both command API and a memoized JSX-producing `DeleteConfirmDialog` callback (lines 48-63), coupling render concerns with imperative mutation logic.
The same hook also performs a manual cast of mutation variables (`as string`, line 64), signaling shape uncertainty at the same boundary.
findings.suggestion: Return pure state/actions from the hook and render a separate `DeleteGenerationConfirmDialog` component from that state; type mutation variables explicitly to remove casts.

findings.summary: Shared-layer tests use conflicting placement conventions, weakening directory ownership signals.
evidence: Component tests are placed beside runtime modules in `src/shared/components/*.test.tsx`.
Hook tests are grouped in `src/shared/hooks/__tests__/`, establishing a different boundary pattern inside the same `shared` package.
This inconsistency makes test ownership/discovery dependent on folder-specific tribal knowledge instead of one predictable convention.
findings.suggestion: Standardize one shared-layer testing layout (either colocated or `__tests__` folders) and apply it across both components and hooks; for example, move component tests into `src/shared/components/__tests__/` and update test globs/import paths accordingly.

findings.summary: Build/tooling configs aggregate unrelated policy domains, obscuring architectural ownership.
evidence: `vite.config.ts` contains local dev networking policy, deployment host allowlist, bundling strategy, logger warning filtering, and dependency tuning in one entry point.
`tailwind.config.ts` contains framework settings plus an extensive brand palette with legacy alias mappings and animation definitions, creating a large mixed-responsibility configuration surface.
Both files act as catch-all locations, so changes in deployment/runtime policy and design-system policy land in the same architectural layer.
findings.suggestion: Split each config by responsibility and import composed modules: e.g., extract Vite server/preview/build/logger sections into `config/vite/*`, and move Tailwind theme tokens/legacy aliases/animations into `src/shared/theme/*` with a single assembly point in `tailwind.config.ts`.

findings.summary: Large orchestrator hooks combine too many responsibilities in one unit.
evidence: `useImageEditOrchestrator` accepts a very wide prop object (lines 32-92), wires multiple mode hooks, and builds a giant `imageEditValue` object plus long dependency list (lines 394-520), coupling composition, mapping, and UI state shaping.

`useTimelineOrchestrator` defines broad interaction/data models (lines 55-158) and owns zoom math, drag lifecycle effects, drop interception, duplicate frame collision logic, structure-video insertion, and model assembly in one hook (lines 314-620).
findings.suggestion: Split each hook into a small coordinator plus domain modules with explicit contracts (for example: `useTimelineViewport`, `useTimelineDropPipeline`, `useTimelineStructureVideoActions`; `useImageEditContextModel`, `useImageEditModeAdapters`). Keep the top-level hook to composition only.

findings.summary: Shot editor controllers rely on oversized parameter bags and pass-through orchestration.
evidence: `UseGenerationControllerParams` has a very large flat contract (lines 7-65), then mostly forwards those values into `useSteerableMotionHandlers` and `useGenerateBatch` (lines 137-220).
`useEditingController` similarly aggregates structure video, audio, name editing, and join setup/handling into one returned mega-object (lines 39-224).
`ShotSettingsContextValue` exposes a broad cross-domain surface (generation mode, join state, structure video, shot management, image handlers), reinforcing a central all-in-one boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed sub-contexts or feature slices (`editing`, `generation`, `join`) and pass a small `ShotEditorRuntime` object per slice instead of flat prop bags. Controllers should own one domain each and expose minimal interfaces.

findings.summary: Form submission flow uses multi-layer wrapper pass-through with low leverage.
evidence: `useFormSubmission` destructures many fields and forwards most of them unchanged to `useTaskParamsBuilder` and `useSubmissionHandlers` (lines 20-108).
`useSubmissionHandlers` defines another broad props interface and forwards overlapping fields into two more hooks (lines 7-78), primarily acting as a routing layer.
`usePromptQueueHandlers` repeats the same prompt-generation dependencies in yet another input shape (lines 8-44), creating a deep handoff path for a single feature.
findings.suggestion: Collapse this chain into one domain hook with internal private helpers, or define a single `FormSubmissionRuntime` object shared by sub-hooks. Avoid repeating near-identical prop surfaces at each layer.

findings.summary: Server handlers mix HTTP concerns and domain/payment orchestration in single files.
evidence: `stripe-checkout` handles CORS/method checks, body validation, env bootstrapping, auth/rate limiting, Stripe session construction, DB lookup, and response mapping in one `serve` body (lines 41-185).

`process-auto-topup` similarly combines request parsing, service auth, eligibility checks, concurrency claim logic, Stripe payment execution, ledger writes, retry/disable logic, and error mapping in one function (lines 36-277).
`generation-child.ts` centralizes child-generation orchestration, migration fallbacks, variant creation paths, and DB lookup strategy in one module with mutable task-param flow (lines 41-260+).
findings.suggestion: Refactor to a pipeline: transport adapter (HTTP), auth/guard layer, application service, and persistence/payment gateways. Keep entry files thin and move business workflows into testable service modules.

findings.summary: Complete-task handlers annotate critical inputs as `unknown` but use them as fully typed objects without runtime schema validation.
evidence: `createGenerationFromTask(..., taskData: unknown, ..., logger?: unknown)` immediately accesses `taskData.params`, `taskData.category`, and `taskData.task_type`.
`handleRegeneration(..., existingGeneration: unknown, ...)` reads `existingGeneration.id` directly.
Orchestrator logic stores `error: unknown` and repeatedly rethrows with `cause: ... as Error`, masking non-Error payloads rather than narrowing.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit task payload/result interfaces (or Zod validators) at the function boundary, narrow once, and pass typed objects through handlers; replace `as Error` with `instanceof Error` fallback wrapping.

findings.summary: Supabase realtime instrumentation bypasses type safety with double-casts to private SDK internals and mutable monkey-patching.
evidence: Instrumentation starts from `const realtime = supabase.realtime as unknown as RealtimeClientWithInstrumentation` and then patches `socket`/`conn.transport` via `Object.defineProperty`.
Custom interface includes non-public fields and methods (`__setAuth`, `conn.transport.ws`), so SDK updates can silently break behavior with no compile error.
findings.suggestion: Wrap realtime access behind a guarded adapter (`isInstrumentableRealtimeClient` type predicate + runtime property checks) and confine private-field usage to one module with explicit fallback behavior.

findings.summary: Broad `Record<string, unknown>` contracts in task/orchestration helpers erase domain shape and propagate unsafe casts across modules.
evidence: `buildOrchestrationContract` returns `Record<string, unknown>` despite producing a stable schema (`contract_version`, `task_family`, etc.).
`payloadBuilder` casts full params to generic records for normalization (`params as Record<string, unknown>`), then returns generic objects used by downstream task handlers.
`initEdgeRuntime` casts `clientOptions` to `never` for `createClient`, suppressing incompatible option shapes at compile time.
findings.suggestion: Define shared typed payload interfaces for orchestration/task params and use them end-to-end; replace generic record returns with concrete types and remove `as never` by typing `clientOptions` to Supabase client options.

findings.summary: Cache read/write paths rely on unchecked casts from `unknown`, so query-shape drift is not caught by TypeScript.
evidence: Preloader cleanup casts query keys and cached state (`query.queryKey as unknown[]`, `query.state?.data as PaginatedResponse | undefined`) before filtering/removal.
Shot editor callbacks mutate cached arrays via `oldData: unknown` and assume item shape `{ id: string }` during filters.
findings.suggestion: Use typed query-key factories and typed `getQueryData`/`setQueryData` helpers per key family so cache payloads are inferred, and introduce runtime guards where mixed legacy shapes are unavoidable.
